charts.title: Topic Map
charts.description: High-level overview of all major topics covered in Week 1.

nodes.label:
Week 1
Non-monotonic Logics
nodes.note: Week 1 introduces classical propositional logic, the failure of monotonicity for real-world reasoning, sequent calculi as proof systems, and the Gabbay-Makinson rules for non-monotonic consequence.

nodes.label:
What is
a Logic?
nodes.note: A logic is identified with a consequence relation between sets of formulas and individual formulas. This relation can be defined semantically (via models) or syntactically (via proof rules).

nodes.label:
Why
Non-monotonic?
nodes.note: Classical logic is monotonic: adding premises never retracts conclusions. The Tweety/penguin example shows how this leads to contradictions when defeasible information is added.

nodes.label:
Sequent
Calculi
nodes.note: Sequent calculi (Gentzen 1935) operate on sequents (pairs of finite sets of formulas) rather than individual formulas, providing an alternative to natural deduction and Hilbert systems.

nodes.label:
Gabbay-Makinson
Rules
nodes.note: The Gabbay-Makinson proof system GM defines a non-monotonic consequence relation snake by seven rules that capture the intuitive notion of usually-follows.

nodes.label:
Derived Rules
of GM
nodes.note: Several useful rules can be derived from the seven basic GM rules without adding expressive power, including superclassicality, conditionalisation, cautious cut, and disjunctive rationality.

nodes.label:
Worked
Examples
nodes.note: Example proofs in GM include the penguin/bird knowledge base, showing how rational consequences can be derived from defeasible premises.

nodes.label:
Syntax vs
Semantics
nodes.note: Semantics defines truth via models and valuations; syntax defines provability via mechanical proof rules. The completeness theorem shows they coincide for classical logic.

nodes.label:
Tweety
Example
nodes.note: The Tweety/penguin example demonstrates that classical logic forces a contradiction (and explosion) when adding the fact that penguins cannot fly to a knowledge base where birds usually fly.

nodes.label:
Monotonicity
Definition
nodes.note: A logic is monotonic if adding premises never invalidates conclusions: Gamma entails F implies Gamma union Delta entails F. Classical propositional and predicate logic are both monotonic.

nodes.label:
Sequent
Rules
nodes.note: The sequent calculus S has reflexivity (REF) as its only axiom, left and right rules for each connective, and structural rules MON and CUT.

nodes.label:
The Seven
GM Rules
nodes.note: The seven basic GM rules: REF, R-and, L-or, LLE (left logical equivalence), RWE (right weakening), CMON (cautious monotonicity), and RMON (rational monotonicity).

nodes.label:
Rational
Monotonicity
nodes.note: RMON allows adding G to the left if not-G does not usually follow. Unlike other rules, it has a negative premise: proving that something does NOT usually follow.

nodes.label:
Penguin/Bird
KB
nodes.note: The penguin knowledge base shows that birds that are penguins usually do not fly, and that birds are usually not penguins, can both be derived as rational consequences.

charts.title: Sequent Calculus Proof Procedure
charts.description: Backward proof search in the sequent calculus S, starting from the goal sequent and applying rules in reverse until all leaves are REF axioms.

nodes.label:
Goal: prove
Gamma => Delta
nodes.note: Begin with the sequent we want to prove. We work backwards from this root, applying sequent rules in reverse to reduce it to simpler sequents until we reach axioms.

nodes.label:
Is the sequent
an instance of REF?
nodes.note: Check whether the current sequent already has the form F => F (some formula on both sides). If so, this branch is closed by the reflexivity axiom and no further work is needed.

nodes.label:
Leaf closed
by REF
nodes.note: The branch terminates with an instance of the axiom schema REF, meaning F => F holds for the formula F that is shared by both sides.

nodes.label:
Pick a non-atomic
formula in sequent
nodes.note: Choose any compound formula in either the left or right of the sequent. Picking different formulas may give simpler or more complex proofs but the choice does not affect provability.

nodes.label:
On left or
on right?
nodes.note: Determine which side of the turnstile the chosen formula is on, since left rules and right rules are different and apply only on their respective sides.

nodes.label:
Apply matching
left rule
nodes.note: Apply the left rule for the connective in the chosen formula (e.g. L-and for conjunction on the left). This produces one or two new sequent premises to prove instead.

nodes.label:
Apply matching
right rule
nodes.note: Apply the right rule for the connective in the chosen formula (e.g. R-and for conjunction on the right). This produces one or two new sequent premises to prove instead.

nodes.label:
All branches
closed by REF?
nodes.note: Check whether every leaf of the proof tree built so far is an instance of REF. If yes the proof is finished; if no there are still open sequents to reduce.

nodes.label: Proof complete
nodes.note: Every leaf of the proof tree is an instance of REF, so the original goal sequent is derivable in the sequent calculus S. By completeness, it is also a logical consequence.

charts.title: Sequent Calculus S - Rules Taxonomy
charts.description: Classification of all the rules of the sequent calculus S for classical propositional logic.

nodes.label:
Sequent
Calculus S
nodes.note: The sequent calculus S adapted from Gentzen 1935. It is sound and complete for classical propositional logic.

nodes.label: Axiom Schema
nodes.note: Rules with zero premises that serve as the leaves of any proof tree.

nodes.label: REF: F => F
nodes.note: The reflexivity axiom: any formula F implies itself, providing the base case for all proofs.

nodes.label: Logical Rules
nodes.note: Rules that introduce or decompose logical connectives, with one left and one right version per connective.

nodes.label: L-and / R-and
nodes.note: Conjunction rules: L-and joins F and G on the left into F-and-G; R-and splits F-and-G on the right into two subgoals.

nodes.label: L-or / R-or
nodes.note: Disjunction rules: L-or splits a disjunction on the left into two cases; R-or joins F and G on the right into F-or-G.

nodes.label: L-not / R-not
nodes.note: Negation rules: move a formula across the turnstile by adding a negation, switching sides between left and right.

nodes.label: L-impl / R-impl
nodes.note: Implication rules: L-impl behaves like L-or (since F implies G is equivalent to not-F or G); R-impl moves the antecedent to the left.

nodes.label: Structural Rules
nodes.note: Rules that manipulate the structure of sequents without dealing with specific connectives.

nodes.label:
MON
(Monotonicity)
nodes.note: Add any extra formulas to the left of an already proven sequent. This directly captures the monotonicity of classical logic.

nodes.label: CUT
nodes.note: Merge two sequents by cutting out a shared formula or list of formulas, removing intermediate lemmas from a proof.

charts.title: Gabbay-Makinson Rules Taxonomy
charts.description: The seven basic GM rules and four key derived rules for the non-monotonic consequence relation snake.

nodes.label:
Gabbay-Makinson
System
nodes.note: GM is the proof system that captures defeasible reasoning syntactically. Its rules describe how the snake (usually-follows) relation should behave.

nodes.label: Basic Rules
nodes.note: The seven primitive rules of GM that define the non-monotonic consequence relation. All other rules can be derived from these.

nodes.label: REF: F snake F
nodes.note: Reflexivity: any formula usually follows from itself. This is the only axiom schema with zero premises.

nodes.label:
R-and: combine
consequents
nodes.note: If G and H both usually follow from F, then their conjunction also usually follows from F.

nodes.label:
L-or: combine
premises
nodes.note: If H usually follows from F and from G separately, then H usually follows from their disjunction.

nodes.label:
LLE: rewrite
left side
nodes.note: If F is classically equivalent to G, then F snake H gives G snake H. The equivalence is a side condition on the rule.

nodes.label:
RWE: weaken
right side
nodes.note: If H is a classical consequence of G, then F snake G gives F snake H. Allows replacing the consequent with a weaker formula.

nodes.label:
CMON: cautious
monotonicity
nodes.note: If F snake G and F snake H, then F-and-G snake H. Adding a conjunct G that already usually holds preserves the conclusion.

nodes.label:
RMON: rational
monotonicity
nodes.note: If F snake H and F does NOT snake not-G, then F-and-G snake H. Has a negative premise unlike the other rules.

nodes.label: Derived Rules
nodes.note: Useful rules that can be proved from the seven basic rules. They simplify proofs but add no new expressive power.

nodes.label:
SC: super-
classicality
nodes.note: Classical consequences are also defeasible: if F entails G classically, then F snake G. Derivable from REF and RWE.

nodes.label:
CON: condi-
tionalisation
nodes.note: If F-and-G snake H then F snake (G to H). Moves a conjunct from the left into an implication on the right.

nodes.label:
CC: cautious
cut
nodes.note: If F snake G and F-and-G snake H then F snake H. Allows removing a redundant intermediate lemma G that was usually true.

nodes.label:
NRAT: negation
rationality
nodes.note: If F snake H and F-and-not-G does NOT snake H, then F-and-G snake H. Distinguishes which conjuncts matter for the conclusion.

charts.title: Penguin Example: b and p snake not-f
charts.description: Backward proof in GM that birds that are penguins usually do not fly, given the penguin knowledge base.

nodes.label:
Goal:
b and p snake not-f
nodes.note: We want to derive that birds-and-penguins usually do not fly, from the premises that penguins usually do not fly, that penguins not-being-birds is impossible, and that birds usually fly.

nodes.label:
Apply LLE:
p and b ~ b and p
nodes.note: Use left logical equivalence: p-and-b is classically equivalent to b-and-p, so it suffices to prove p and b snake not-f.

nodes.label:
Apply CMON
to get p and b snake not-f
nodes.note: Cautious monotonicity adds a conjunct that already usually holds. We need the two premises p snake not-f and p snake b.

nodes.label:
p snake not-f
(premise 2)
nodes.note: This is one of the original defeasible premises in the penguin knowledge base: penguins usually do not fly. It serves as a leaf of the proof tree.

nodes.label:
Need:
p snake b
nodes.note: We must show that penguins usually are birds. This is not a direct premise so we derive it from premise 3: penguins that are not birds are impossible.

nodes.label:
Apply CON
to premise 3
nodes.note: From premise 3 p-and-not-b snake bottom, conditionalisation gives p snake (not-b to bottom). This moves the conjunct not-b into an implication on the right.

nodes.label:
Apply RWE:
not-b to bottom |= b
nodes.note: Classically, not-b implies bottom is logically equivalent to b. By right weakening we conclude p snake b, completing the proof of premise b for CMON.

nodes.label:
b and p snake not-f
proved
nodes.note: All branches of the proof tree are either premises or have been derived. The conclusion is therefore a rational consequence of the penguin knowledge base.
